content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Mary L. McMaster 
General Counsel 
Goleta Water District 
336 East Pedregosa Street 
Santa Barbara, CA 9310 I 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-270 
Dear Ms. McMaster: 
February 8, 2017 
You are the General Counsel for the Goleta Water District ("Water District") and are 
writing on behalf of Goleta Water District Board Member Meg West. This letter responds to your 
request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the 
"Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
QUESTIONS 
1. May Water District Director Meg West participate in a decision before the Water District 
Board that would allow irrigation of areas planted entirely with lawns or turf grasses that are 
on a list of low water use grasses approved by the Water District during a Stage 4 Water 
Shortage Emergency? 
2. May Water District Director West participate in a decision before the Water District to 
declare a Stage 4 Water Shortage Emergency? 
CONCLUSION 
1 - 2. No. Water District Director Meg West may not participate in the decisions because it 
is reasonably foreseeable that there will be a material financial effect upon her financial interests. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-16-270 
Page No. 2 
Water District Director Meg West owns and operates a landscape design business, Meg 
West Design, which specializes in sustainable landscape design. One of the common features of 
sustainable landscape design is the replacement of turf grass with drought-tolerant plants and other 
drought-tolerant landscape features. Although Director West is not currently taking on new clients, 
she continues to do landscape design work for existing clients. 
Due to the drought on the Central Coast, the Water District has declared a Stage 3 Water 
Shortage Emergency. If the drought continues, the Water District will likely consider declaration of 
a Stage 4 Water Shortage Emergency during the 2017 calendar year. One of the strategies the Water 
District uses during water shortages is to encourage customers to conserve water through measures 
that include restricting the times when outdoor irrigation is permitted, encouraging customers to 
switch from turf grass to drought-tolerant landscaping and, in later stages, prohibiting the watering 
of turf grass. In Stage 4, irrigation through the use of a sprinkler is prohibited as is irrigation of 
lawns except in special circumstances. 
There are two matters that are likely to come before the Water District Board of directors in 
the ^ear future that may implicate conflict of interest concen:is for Director West. 
The District is considering an exception to the general prohibition on lawn watering in Stage 
4 that would allow irrigation of areas planted entirely with lawns or turf grasses that are included on 
a list of low water use grasses approved by the Water District. This exemption would allow 
customers to maintain lawns and grass areas in a Stage 4 Water Shortage Emergency so long as 
those areas were planted with certain designated species of low water use grasses. 
You indicate that one could argue that such a code change could possibly increase the 
demand for landscape architects, such as Director West, who could then assist clients with selecting 
low water use grasses and designing landscapes to accommodate them. Director West would like 
assistance from the FPPC in determining whether she could participate in formulating and voting on 
a code change exempting water wise grasses from prohibitions that would otherwise apply. 
As mentioned above the Water District may be faced with the decision of whether to declare 
a Stage 4 Water Shortage Emergency in the near future. Since the use of outdoor sprinkler systems 
is prohibited in Stage 4, the declaration of Stage 4 could motivate some Water District customers to 
replace lawns and other landscape features that require overhead watering with landscape features 
that either do not require watering or that can be watered using a drip irrigation system. It is 
possible that customers faced with that decision might be likely to employ the services of a 
landscape architect to help them transition their landscape to one compatible with Stage 4 
requirements. Director West would like assistance from the FPPC in determining whether or not she 
can vote on a Stage 4 Water shortage declaration. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will "perform their 
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial 
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Section 87100 prohibits any 


File No. A-16-270 
Page No. 3 
public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position 
to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. The Commission 
has adopted a standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of 
interest. (Regulation 18700(b).) However, the general rule is that a conflict of interest exists 
whenever a public official makes a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. 
Your letter acknowledges that Director West is a public official who, by virtue of her 
position on the Water District Board, will participate in decisions concerning the allowance of 
exceptions to irrigation prohibitions during a State 4 Water Shortage Emergency and also whether 
to declare a Stage 4 Water Shortage Emergency in the first place. (Steps One and Two). In addition, 
you have identified Director West's interest in her business as the potentially affected financial 
interest (Step Three). 
In the case of a business entity interest, the financial effect of the governmental decision is 
deemed material only under certain circumstances that do not apply here. (See Regulation 
18702.l(a).) However, Regulation 18702.l(b) in relevant part provides: 
"For a governmental decision not identified in subdivision (a), the financial effect 
is material if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the decision's financial effect would contribute to a 
change in the price of the business entity's publicly traded stock, or the value of a 
privately-held business entity. Examples of decisions that may be applicable 
include those that: 
"(3) Increase or decrease the need for the products or services that the business 
entity supplies ... " 
Regulation 18705.1 (b) thus applies a "prudent person" standard looking to all of the 
surrounding factors to determine whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the financial effect of a 
governmental decision is material. For the effect of a decision to be "reasonably foreseeable," it 
need not be likely but only needs to be "recognized as a realistic possibility and more than 
hypothetical or theoretical ... ., (Regulation l 8706(b ). ) 
Director West owns a business which specializes in sustainable landscape design. If the 
Water District Board were to decide to declare a Stage 4 Water Shortage Emergency, residents in 
the jurisdiction would be prohibited from irrigation through the use of a sprinkler except in special 
circumstances their yard in order to conserve water. This would likely cause an increase in demand 
for sustainable, low water usage landscapes as sprinkler systems that were currently in place would 
no longer be an allowable use. The Stage 4 Water Shortage Emergency rating would create new 
conditions that would affect how Director West's business would be able to operate and the 
products it would be able to provide. The restrictions on irrigation that would be imposed as a result 
of declaring a Stage 4 Water Shortage Emergency would likely increase competition for Director 
West's business as other landscape businesses may decide to also provide similar services due to 
the increased demand for sustainable landscape design. Additionally, a decision to create exceptions 
that would allow for irrigation oflow water use lawns or turf grasses during a Stage 4 Water 
Shortage Emergency would also create greater demand for these types of sustainable landscapes. 


File No. A-16-270 
Page No. 4 
Accordingly, there will be a reasonably foreseeable material financial affect upon Director 
West's financial interest in her business. Therefore, she should not participate in either of the 
decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
SKB:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
/JH,,!t 
Sukhi K. Brar 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
